text: Running-related injury risk factors span multiple domains including running/training characteristics, health and lifestyle factors, and morphological and biomechanical aspects, confirming the multifactorial basis of injury incidence.
Running and training characteristics exhibit large effect sizes for increasing risk of running-related injuries, making training load management a primary modifiable risk factor.
Health and lifestyle factors show large effect sizes for running-related injury risk, indicating that overall health status and lifestyle habits significantly influence injury susceptibility.
Morphological and biomechanical aspects exhibit large effect sizes for running-related injury risk, suggesting structural and movement-pattern screening may help identify at-risk runners.

Among reported risk factor associations for running-related injuries, 23% showed large effect sizes, 29% medium, 37% small, and 11% no effect, indicating most identified risk factors have at least some measurable association with injury.

The overall quality of systematic reviews on running-related injury risk factors is critically low to low, meaning individual risk factor findings should be interpreted cautiously and not treated as definitive.
